location.name: The Bathroom
location.text: The bathroom contains an enamelled cast iron bath with claw feet and a toilet bearing a brass tablet calling it 'The Imperial Avenger'. A washstand with a marble top and a pedestal washbowl complete the ensemble. Various toileteries are scattered around in the normal way.

location.name: The Orchard
location.text: The park wall lies north and has several pear-trees trained in espalier against it. There are a large number of apple trees, but no fruit. There are various marks on the ground, too unclear to be sure that they are footprints.

location.name: The Kitchen Garden
location.text: The kitchen garden is full of frozen vegetables lying under snow. Various footprints are visible in the snow.

location.name: Outhouses
location.text: This section of the garden contains various outhouses; a laundry, a coal store and a pigeon-coop.

location.name: The Ornamental Pond
location.text: An ornamental pond stands at the western end of the terrace. The pond is covered in ice. In the four corners of the pond are stone pedestals. These hold statues.

location.name: The Terrace
location.text: From the house a french window leads onto this paved terrace, which shows traces of many footprints in the snow. The french window is open and has a broken pane.

location.name: Colonel Darnley's Study
location.text: Colonel Darnley's body lies on a turkish carpet in the middle of the study. A port glass is by his body and a tantalus of port stands on a table. The room is panelled and contains several club-chairs, some glass cases holding stuffed birds and a writing table with a letter on it as well as various sporting magazines and a rack of sporting guns and pistols. As you walk on the floor by the french window in the west wall, you feel broken glass underfoot.

location.name: The Hall
location.text: The walls of the hall are decorated with swords and Civil War armour. The floor is marble. The only furniture is a glass case containing various flints and shards of pottery. At the far end of the hall, two sets of fine armour stand either side of the stairs which have barley-sugar bannisters and carved pineapple finials.

location.name: The Kitchen
location.text: The kitchen has game birds hanging from the ceiling and the usual kitchen furniture. On the sink draining-board is a knife. By the outside door is a line of boots and some cleaning brushes and polish.

location.name: Butlers' Pantry
location.text: The butlers' pantry is laid out as a sitting room with a bed behind a curtain. Two temporary beds have been made up. There is a small bookshelf.

location.name: The Maze
location.text: Situated on the east side of the house, the maze consists of low box hedges. The marks in the snow cannot be deciphered into footprints.

location.name: West Side of the House
location.text: The gravel path around the house passes between the smooth snow covered tennis court and a bed of withered flowers covered in snow. Various footprints are visible in the snow.

location.name: The Lounge
location.text: The centrepiece of the lounge is a three piece suite on a Chinese carpet. There are several tables holding magazines and novels marked with bookmarks. Recent family portraits are on the walls. The fireplace is carved and holds the remains of a fire. On the mantlepiece is an Indian rosewood box with an ivory inlay.

location.name: The Dining Room
location.text: The dining room holds a fine oak table with chairs in William and Mary style. A glass case contains various pieces of Delft pottery. A sideboard holding a silver wine cooler and a pair of Sevres porcellain urns stand against one wall. The fireplace is of carved marble and the walls hold various Italian landscapes.

location.name: The Music Room
location.text: In the centre of the music room is a piano and, on top, a brass ashtray with the stub of a cigar in it. There are several chairs, a music stand and in one corner a large harp with broken strings.

location.name: The Billiard Room
location.text: A full size table occupies the centre and of the wall a board indicates the result of a game. There is a small bookcase holding magazines and several novels by Conan Doyle and Buchan. On top of the book- case are three wine glasses and an ashtray.

location.name: The Drive
location.text: From a pair of splendid wrought iron gates the drive runs north ending in a flight of steps leading up to the principal entrance of the house, flanked by a pair of stone lions. The snow reveals a mass of footprints.

location.name: Avenue of Statues
location.text: This gravel path runs east-west across the front of the house flanked by an avenue of statues. The snow covered gravel is too rough to distinguish footprints.

location.name: The Summer House
location.text: A wooden summer house stands on a patch of lawn. Inside can be seen some croquet mallets and hoops, several cigarette ends and a handkerchief.

location.name: West Lawn
location.text: Between the summer house and the drive is a lawn. It is planted with a few saplings and the covering of snow shows footprints.

location.name: The Stables
location.text: The stables are falling into disrepair. One part contains Colonel Darnley's bay hunter. The other garages a large Daimler. The yard is full of straw and oil spills, and is crossed by footprints. The tack room holds the saddle and gear for the horse.

location.name: East Lawn
location.text: An area of lawn between the stables and the cottage. The smooth covering of snow is broken by footprints.

location.name: Gamekeeper's Cottage
location.text: In the damp shade of some chestnut trees is a gamekeeper's cottage tucked against the south-east corner of the park. The door of the cottage is open and footprints are visible across the threshold. Inside are various items of game, two shotguns on the wall, a table covered by oil cloth, an unmade bed, a slop-stone sink and an open fire with a pot hanging over it. The place is very dirty; the hearth is littered with foodscraps, the ends of cigarettes and newspapers.

location.name: Milicent Darnley's Bedroom
location.text: Milicent Darnley's bedroom is decorated to a young lady's taste with a bed, wardrobe, dressing table and a small writing table. The surfaces are covered with small ornaments, mainly of dogs and the walls have pictures of favourite pets.

location.name: The Landing
location.text: The landing walls hold some fine antlers and animal heads and some bad portraits of the Darnley ancestors.

location.name: The Servants' Quarters
location.text: The servants' quarters consist of a single room with two single beds, a rail for hanging clothes on behind a curtain, a wash stand and a chest of drawers. On the chest is a bundle of correspondence and various framed photographs. A gentleman's country-suit with a newspaper in the pocket is hanging on the rail amongst the dresses and a pipe is on the washstand.

location.name: The Master Bedroom
location.text: Colonel and Mrs. Darnley's bedroom is heavily panelled and reflects the Colonel's tastes, with sporting prints and a humidor of cigars. There is a double bed, a dressing table, wardrobe and trouser press. A small bathroom is attached.

location.name: The Morning Room
location.text: The morning room holds a chintz sofa and an occasional table with magazines and books. Against one wall is a piano. On a table stands a gramophone. There are various pictures, mainly watercolours of birds.

location.name: Guest Room
location.text: A guest-room decorated in blue with wardrobe, bed, washstand and writing-table. By the bed is a pair of men's slippers. On the writing table is a pack of cigarettes and some stationary and pens.

location.name: Gravel Walk
location.text: The gravel walk is covered by snow too broken to show the traces of footprints.

location.name: South-East Corner of the House
location.text: At the south-east corner of the house is a square of empty lawn. The smooth cover of snow is broken by footprints.

person.name: Colonel Darnley
person.text: Aged 60. The Colonel served in the South African War and contracted enteric fever which left him feeling debiliated and short- tempered. He had only a small inheritance but made a fortune on the stock exchange. He was free with his tips on the market, but didn't always follow his own recommendations.

person.name: Sarah Darnley
person.text: Colonel Darnley's wife. Aged 27. Fair-haired, beautiful and sophisticated. She wears a morning dress and shows signs of tired- ness and stress.

person.name: Millicent Darnley
person.text: Colonel Darnley's daughter. Aged 23. Well-dressed but rather plain. Cool and determined looking. She wears a blouse and skirt.

person.name: Arthur Coniston
person.text: Millicent Darnley's fiance. Aged 25. Average height and very good looking. His manner is honest and friendly. He dresses fashionably and wears black patent shoes.

person.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
person.text: Friend of the deceased. Aged 52. Very tall and boney with side whiskers and a red face. Wears country-tweeds and boots. His manner is ill-tempered.

person.name: Arnold Billingsgate
person.text: The Butler. Aged about 60; gentlemanly manners; bald and thin; average height. Wears a tail coat and ordinary men's shoes.

person.name: Mildred Goodbody
person.text: The Cook. Aged 55. Pleasant-faced. Cheerful. Overweight.

person.name: Norah Bagsby
person.text: The Housemaid. Aged 19. Small pale girl who sniffs a lot. Wears a maid's uniform.

person.name: Ronald Mellors
person.text: The Gamekeeper. Stocky, black curly hair, good looking, surly- looking. Wears working clothes and hob-nailed boots. Age 35.

item.name: Pond
item.text: Near the centre of the pond the ice is thinner as if it has frozen again after having been broken. Beneath the ice you can see the missing statue from the fourth pedestal.

item.name: Sunken Statue
item.text: The statue that is beneath the ice is the same as those on the pedestals. Stuck in a crevice of the statue is the soaked butt of a cheroot.

item.name: Revolver
item.text: The revolver found in the hall is a Webley-Fosbery .455 service revolver. One bullet has been discharged from it.

item.name: Statues
item.text: The statues form a set of four, representing the Four Seasons. They are of Italian marble and rest on their own weight rather than being cemented to their plinths. Some strength would be required to move one.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: Only one set of footprints can be made out in the snow. They are of flat footed bootprints coming to and from the terrace.

item.name: Slippers
item.text: The slippers are slightly damp and stained with mud.

item.name: Cigarettes
item.text: These cigarettes are an expensive brand by Tribourg & Freyer.

item.name: Knife
item.text: This kitchen knife is stained with blood.

item.name: Boots
item.text: All these boots are polished clean except for a mud stained pair that apparently belong to Sir Redvers Slingsby.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: Both flat footed boot prints and slipper prints can be seen going between the kitchen and the maze.

item.name: Bed
item.text: This double bed has not been slept in.

item.name: Wardrobe
item.text: As well as clothes the wardrobe contains a selection of men's and women's shoes.

item.name: Cigars
item.text: The cigars are Burmah cheroots.

item.name: Dressing Table
item.text: There is nothing of note on the dressing table.

item.name: Shoes
item.text: This selection of mens and womens shoes is unremarkable. One set of lady's shoes is mud-stained.

item.name: Correspondence
item.text: These letters are all from Mrs. Goodbody's nephew.

item.name: Photographs
item.text: These photographs depict Mrs. Goodbody's various ugly relatives.

item.name: Suit
item.text: This gentleman's country-suit is apparently Sir Redvers'.

item.name: Newspaper
item.text: One headline on the front page catches your eye - 'Collapse of Rio Plata Mining Co. - Our co-respondent in Lima, Peru reports the collapse of several silver mining companies through the fraud of their directors. These frauds are expected to cause heavy loss to British investors.'

item.name: Pipe
item.text: The pipe is in the style called 'calabash' with a meerschaum bowl and amber mouthpiece.

item.name: Bookshelf
item.text: The bookshelf contains books on self-improvement and domestic accounts.

item.name: French Window
item.text: One of the panes is smashed, but there is no glass on the terrace. The window lock shows that it cannot be locked or unlocked from outside.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: A mess of footprints including boots, slippers and some shoe prints.

item.name: Colonel Darnley's Body
item.text: The body is dressed in a smoking jacket open at the chest. There is a bloody bullet wound in the front of his dress-shirt. The bullet has not passed through the body. Evidently the Colonel had got out of his chair on hearing or seeing his murderer. The impact of the bullet had caused him to spin and fall and the furniture had got in the way, further deflecting the body. It is no longer possible to tell the direction of the shot.

item.name: Port Glass
item.text: The port glass is one of a set engraved with the words 'Floreat Darnley'. A crust of dried liquid is at the bottom and lip marks on the rim though no trace of lipstick.

item.name: Tantalus of Port
item.text: The tantalus is made of cherrywood. It holds an empty decanter with a silver label marked 'Brandy' and a half full decanter marked 'Port'. The decanters are normally inaccessible since they are held in place by a sort of 'lid' of wood which is fastened by a small lock. The key is in the lock.

item.name: Letter
item.text: The letter is an incomplete draft : 'Dear Sir, I have considered your monstrous suggestion and find I cannot....''

item.name: Rack of Guns
item.text: One of the pistols is missing from this rack.

item.name: French Window
item.text: One pane is smashed. The glass on the floor is apparently from this.

item.name: Glass
item.text: This glass is apparently from the smashed French Window.

item.name: Gramophone
item.text: It has a device for playing several records in sequence. All the records are Chopin.

item.name: Piano
item.text: There are several sheets of Schubert Lied music on the piano's music stand.

item.name: Display Case
item.text: According to a label at the front of the case these flints and shards of pottery were found in the grounds of the house.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: There are slipper prints, flat-footed boot prints and some shoe prints heading to and from the terrace and the the front of the house.

item.name: Cigar Stub
item.text: This cigar stub is a straight dark cylinder rather than the conventional shape. That and the tarry appearance suggests a cheroot.

item.name: Piano
item.text: The sheets of music on the piano are for duets.

item.name: Wine Glasses
item.text: The three wine glasses are all dirty.

item.name: Ashtray
item.text: The ashtray contains the remains of three cigars, one Havana and two cheroots.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: The prints from flat footed boots go east and west along the gravel path in front of the house. Slipper prints come from the house and head east and west along the path. Prints from men's shoes come from the house and go west. Prints from lady's shoes head to and from the frontdoor and the west lawn. At the gate end of the drive the prints from men's hobnailed boots go between the west lawn and the stables.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: The prints from flat footed boots and men's slippers are visible in the snow heading around the corner of the house, to and from the maze and avenue of statues.

item.name: Cigarette Ends
item.text: These cigarette ends come from a cheap, mass produced brand.

item.name: Handkerchief
item.text: This is a lady's hanky, plain lace edged and perfumed.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: Prints from lady's shoes and men's hob-nailed boots can be seen in the snow going between the drive and the summer house.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: The prints from hob-nailed boots can be seen in the snow going between the drive and the east lawn.

item.name: Footprints
item.text: The prints from hob-nailed boots can be seen in the snow going east to west between the stables and the cottage.

item.name: Cigarette Ends
item.text: These cigarette ends come from a cheap, mass produced brand.

item.name: Armour
item.text: A suit of Civil War three-quarter armour with a closed helmet. Examination discovers a revolver concealed in the helmet.

item.name: Door Mat
item.text: Some blurb about a damp doormat.

record.name: What really happened at Darnley Park

text.p: On the morning of the murder Arthur Coniston, the boy-friend of Milicent Darnley, arrived by arrangement to ask Colonel Darnley for permission to marry his daughter. This conversation took place during the afternoon and Colonel Darnley refused the request. Although he found Coniston amusing, he felt that his visitor was after Milicent's inheritance. Coniston, now resolved upon murder, quietly informing the other members of the household that the Colonel had agreed to tbe marriage. He was unaware that the Colonel had decided to write him a letter confirming his rejection. During the afternoon an unexpected visitor arrived, Sir Redvers Slingsby who had invested in Peruvian mining shares on the advice of his friend Colonel Darnley. In the morning's papers was news of the collapse of these shares. Angry with his friend, Slingsby had rushed down to Darnley Park to ask the Colonel to lend him money to avoid ruin.

Dinner that evening finished at 9.00. Milicent and Sarah retired to the lounge for coffee while the men drank port and played billiards. At nine 9.30 the gentlemen joined the ladies while the servants ate in the kitchen. The atmosphere was tense since Slingsby had not had opportunity to raise his problems. At 10.00 that night impatience got the better of him and he drew Darnley to the study whilst Coniston and Milicent went to the music room to sing duets. For half an hour Slingsby and the Colonel quarelled (at this time Billingsgate was cleaning boots in the kitchen) and at 10.30 Slingsby stormed out of the study by the hall door, slamming it furiously. This noise was interpreted by some as a shot. The slamming of the door underlined the generally tense atmosphere. The ladies went upstairs but Arthur Coniston stayed in the music room smoking a cheroot and considering his plan. He was unaware that Sarah had plans of her own.

Sarah Darnley was unhappy with her husband and having an affair with Mellors, the gamekeeper. She had arranged to meet Mellors at the summer house at 10.45. Mellors was there, waiting and smoking cigarettes. Sarah took steps to keep the affair secret and to maintain to Milicent the pretence that she was in the house, she told her of her intention to play on the morning room piano. In truth she played a series of gramophone records which Milicent heard from her room. It was not necessary for her to take account of Colonel Darnley since experience had taught her that the Colonel would lock himself in his study and get drunk on such an occasion as this. Sarah next left the house and went down to the summer house where she met Mellors.

Leaving the music room, Coniston tried the study door and finding it locked, guessed what was happening and devised his plan. He had stolen a gun from the study earlier in the day. He now changed into dressing gown and slippers and went outside the house and around to the kitchen door which was open. Reaching through the door he picked up Sir Redvers Slingsby's boots. Changing into them he walked around the house to the terrace. The boots being too large, they left flat-footed bootprints. From the terrace Coniston looked into the study where the Colonel was drinking. He tapped on the window, the Colonel stood up and saw him, Coniston fired the gun and the Colonel fell dead. He now had to act fast to dispose of the gun, disguise of the fact of the bullet-hole in the window glass and return the boots. He broke the ice on the pond with a statue taken from one of the pedestals and threw the gun into the water. He then returned to the kitchen, changed the boots for the slippers and then entered the house by the front door. He did not notice Sarah who had heard the shot and rushed back before everyone was roused by the noise and she was discovered. In her haste Sarah dropped a handkerchief in the summer house.

Coniston now came to the boldest part of his plot. He woke Billingsgate and persuaded him to break in by the french window instead of searching for his keys. Coniston took care to reach the patio before his companion and threw a stone through the same pane of glass that bore the bullet-hole from the fatal shot, thus obliterating the evidence of from where the shooting took place. He then invited Billingsgate to open the window and confirm that it had been locked. He also told the Butler that he intended to follow the murderer's bootprints which were clear in the snow. The object of this move was to provide an explanation of the trail already left by his slippers when he first stole and returned the boots.

Various minor clues led towards Coniston as the murderer, but one was fatal to him. It was evident that the murderer had worn the boots and returned them to the kitchen. How then had the murderer left the kitchen in his/her own footwear? There were only two possibilities : the murderer had left via the butlers' pantry or the outside door. The former was impossible since three people were occupying the pantry of whom at least one (Mrs. Goodbody) had been wakened by the shot and would have heard any stranger entering the room. The second was possible but the murderer would leave the foot- prints of his ordinary footwear. Other than the boots, there were prints only from Arthur Coniston's slippers. Coniston's explanation that he had left these prints in following the murderers boot-trail would not hold water. The murderer had to leave footprints in two sets of footwear. Coniston's slippers were the only other set.
You have been summoned to Darnley Park one winter's day to investigate the murder of Colonel Sebastian Darnley at some time the previous evening. The suspects are;

ul.li: Sir Redvers Slingsby - friend of the deceased

text.p: You are free to search the house and grounds and to question the suspects. Examination of the scene and skillful interrogation will yield sufficient information to solve the mystery. And mystery it is!

The body of Colonel Darnley was found in his study, the doors and window of which were all locked from the inside. The only keys were in the possession of the Colonel and his faithful butler. How could the sealed-room murder be comitted?

I married Sebastian two years ago. I am more than twenty years younger than he - really not much older than Milicent. We have been very happy together. Last night we all had dinner together. After- wards the men played billiards while Milicent and I had coffee in the lounge. At about 9.30 the men joined us for half an hour then Arthur and Milicent said that they were going to the music room. Sebastian and Sir Redvers had business to talk about so they went into the study. I heard them quarelling but couldn't tell what about. Next there was a loud noise which I took to be Sir Redvers leaving the study by the door to the hall and banging the door. I stayed in the lounge for ten minutes and then went upstairs where I ran into Milicent on the landing. She said that she had left Arthur in the billiard room and was going to bed. I went into the morning room and played some Chopin nocturnes on the piano there until about 11.30 when I heard a noise like a shot which I thought was poachers. Looking at my watch I decided it was time to go to bed. I was getting changed when I heard Billingsgate rush upstairs to wake Sir Redvers. Milicent heard the racket and came into my room from hers. And the rest you know.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: At 10.30 I was reading and drinking coffee in the lounge - on my own, I'm afraid. At 11.30 I was playing the piano in the morning room - also alone, alas! I don't suppose it looks to good?

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I don't know whose slippers these are.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the knife.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I can't imagine why you are asking me this question. Arthur Coniston smokes cigarettes.

interrogation.name: The Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't recognise it.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: I believe a rather nasty tramp, who called two days ago threw it into the pond.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: The gramophone? I was in the morning room from about 10.40 until 11.30 playing the piano. It's possible I played a record, but I don't remember doing so.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: If you mean the morning room piano, I was playing it from about 10.40 until I heard the bang at 11.30. I was on my own throughout and no-one else came into the room.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: Yes, these are my shoes, but I don't recall when I last wore them. I have no idea why they should be damp - possibly I had them on earlier yesterday.

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I believe that Sebastian advised one or two people to invest in Peruvian shares.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: My husband said something to me about having to write a letter. I understood it was for Arthur - or was it Sir Redvers? Afterwards Arthur told me that Sebastian had agreed to the marriage, and Redvers said that he and Sebastian had sorted out their business; so I'm at a loss about the letter.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: You found it in the summer house? I'm absolutely sure it isn't mine.

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: He was somewhat strict and suspicous by nature, but we were happy enough.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: Her father spoiled her so she got used to having her own way. Sebastian probably agreed to Arthur Coniston's proposal for fear of what Milicent would do if he refused. I can't see him crossing Milicent on a matter like that.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: A charmer. I was suspicous of his motives for marrying Milicent, but Arthur said that when he asked Sebastian my husband agreed, so I am probably mistaken.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I felt he had some sort of grudge against Sebastian.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: Very honest and loyal.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: A gossip.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: Lazy.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: A very loyal gamekeeper.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I'm too distressed to talk about this.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I'm too distressed to talk about this.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: I'm too distressed to talk about this.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I'm too distressed to talk about this.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I'm too distressed to talk about this.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

text.p: Father was a difficult man to live with but I never expected - this! Last night we all ate together until coffee which Sarah and I took together in the lounge. The men joined us later for half an hour, then Arthur and I went into the music room where we talked for a while and played duets on the piano. At about 10.30 I decided to go to bed. I know the time because I looked at my watch when there was a loud noise - probably a poacher somewhere. Arthur stayed in the billiard room and I went upstairs. I think I saw Sir Redvers disappearing into his bedroom, but I was distracted by Sarah who was on the landing. She said she was intending to play the piano in the morning room and hoped it would't disturb me. I went to my room and stayed there. I heard Sarah playing the piano then drifted off to sleep. I woke up at about 11.20 by the bedside clock and heard Sarah still playing a Chopin nocturne. She seemed to be having trouble since the same part was being repeated over and over again. I went to sleep for a few more minutes when there was a bang - a door slamming, I thought. Then at about midnight there was a racket of people everywhere. I went into Sarah's room and found her undressing there. We went into the morning room and found Billingsgate waking Sir Redvers.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: I was with Arthur in the music room at 10.30 and, naturally, I was alone in bed at 11.30.

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I don't know whose slippers these are.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about these boots.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the knife.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I don't smoke. My father smoked cigars.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't recognise it.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: Someone - I forget who - told me that a beggar had pushed it into the pond. Out of spite I suppose.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: We have a gramophone in the morning room.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: Which piano? Arthur and I played duets on the music room piano for a while after dinner. I remember that later, Sarah said she intended to play the morning room piano. I woke up at about 11.20 and heard Sarah still playing a Chopin nocturne. She was having difficulty and repeated the same piece again and again.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: They aren't my shoes.

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I don't read the newspaper frequently.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about this letter.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: I'm always losing handkerchiefs so I suppose it could be mine. I don't remember when I lose them. I certainly wasn't in the summer house yesterday. Is it important?

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: Daddy was a tyrant but always kind to me. He was always fearful that Sarah would leaver him for a younger man.

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: We got on well enough though it is difficult to have a step-mother who is almost the same age. I thought she would leave daddy some day as soon as she found a lover. But, of course, she would need Daddy's money.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: He's a darling! I don't know how he persuaded daddy to agree, so I am probably mistaken.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I didn't know him before last night.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: A decent old fellow.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: An old dear.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: A clumsy girl.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: Furtive. I don't like the way he looks at my step-mother. I advised Daddy to get rid of him.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: Oh really, it's too silly to ask me to recognise footprints.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: Oh really, it's too silly to ask me to recognise footprints.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: Oh really, it's too silly to ask me to recognise footprints.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: Oh really, it's too silly to ask me to recognise footprints.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: Oh really, it's too silly to ask me to recognise footprints.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

text.p: I was down for the weekend. It was no secret that I was going to ask Colonel Darnley for permission to marry Milicent. I popped the question to the old boy in the afternoon and he agreed. Last night we all had dinner together and afterwards we fellows played a little billiards before joining the ladies for coffee. At about 10.00 Sir Redvers broke up the party by asking Darnley to go into the study with him. I asked Milicent to come with me to the music room where we played duets for half an hour. There was a noise. Milicent thought it was a poacher and hoped that Mellor's would catch him. She decided to go to bed. I stayed on to smoke a cigar and didn't go up until 11.00. I noticed nothing odd except that to get to my bedroom I had to go through the morning room where the gramophone was playing a Chopin nocturne but there was no-one there. I undressed and went to bed but couldn't sleep. At about 11.30 I heard a noise - a door banging, so I thought at the time. I decided to go downstairs and smoke another cigar in the lounge. I noticed a light under the study door and knocked on it to see who was there. I got worried when it was locked and no-one answered so I got Billingsgate and together we went outside and round to the french window. It was locked too, so I picked a stone up and broke the glass. Billingsgate stuck his hand through the hole and tried the lock. He told me it was locked on the inside, and - damn me! - he was right. We both went inside and found Darnley on the floor, shot and dead. When we checked the doors to the hall and lounge we found the Colonel's keys in the locks, so it would have been impossible to open them from the outside. I had noticed some bootprints outside, so I went out and followed them. They led around the house to the kitchen door. I followed in my slippers.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: Milicent and I were together in the music room at 10.30. I don't know how anyone can be expected to have an alibi for 11.30. Certainly a single gentleman could never admit to one, if you follow my meaning.

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: These are my slippers. I was wearing them when Billingsgate and I broke into the study.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the boots.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the knife.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I smoke and expensive cigarette made by Fribourg & Treyer in London - those and cigars after dinner, Cheroots same as old Darnley. I would never smoke cheap cigarettes.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't recognise it.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: I don't know what you're talking about.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: When I went to bed at 11.00, the gramophone was playing a Chopin nocturne. Otherwise the morning room was empty. Later the record appeared to be stuck. It was still playing when I went downstairs shortly after 11.30.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: After dinner Milicent and I played duets on the piano in the music room. Ah - now that I remember, there is also a piano in the morning room, but on the couple of occasions I went into the room there was no-one there, just a gramophone playing.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: I don't know whose shoes these are.

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I heard that old Slingsby had plunged into mining shares and needed money urgently. Colonel Darnley mentioned it to me during our conversation.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: I have no idea who old Darnley was writing to. I suppose it could have been to old Slingsby. I fancy he had lost a lot of money and needed a loan. We had spoken in the afternoon and Darnley had agreed that I could marry Milicent.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: I don't know whose handkerchief it is.

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: A decent old buffer. When I asked his permission to marry Milicent he agreed straight away. I was suprised, I admit it!

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: Very lively. I couldn't see old Darnley hanging on to her.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: Be a gentleman, old fellow! Don't ask me to criticise my future wife.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I scarcely know him but he seemed very angry with Darnley about something. I got the feeling he had money troubles. A dangerous fellow I should think.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: I barely know the chap.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: I don't know the woman.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: I don't know the girl.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: I don't know the chap.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I followed the Bootprints from the terrace around to the kitchen.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose they are.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: The prints from the slippers are mine.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I think they are probably Billingsgate's.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: I suppose it could be mine. I fancy I was smoking when Billingsgate and I went outside. Maybe I did throw it in the pond. Does it matter?

text.p: I have known Darnley for about 20 years. It was some confidential business dealings that brought me here urgently yesterday. Last night I had dinner with the family and young Coniston.

Afterwards about 9.00 - we gentlemen had a game and some port in the billiard room, then we joined the ladies in the lounge for half an hour. Darnley and I left the others at 10.00 and chatted about our business for a while. The discussion was very friendly and I left Darnley alive at 10.30. I was sleeping in the servants quarters and went to bed straight away. Having had a bit to drink I slept very soundly and heard nothing until Billingsgate woke me at midnight to tell me about this terrible business.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: I left Darnley alive at 10.30 and it's damn nonsense to suggest I shot him with all the house awake and then waited to see what would happen. At 11.30 I was asleep like a good Christian, and the Devil take you if a fellow needs an alibi!

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I don't know whose slippers these are.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: These are certainly my boots. I last wore them yesterday afternoon and put them out for Billingsgate to clean. I certainly didn't wear them after dinner and can't explain who may have. I suppose that, if the staff were asleep, someone could have sneaked into the kitchen and taken them.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the knife.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I smoke a pipe and the occasional Havana cigar. My pipe is in my room.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't recognise it.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: I don't know what you're talking about.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: The Darnley's have a gramophone in the morning room.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: The Darnley's have two pianos, one in the music room and one in the morning room.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: I don't know whose shoes these are.

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I was reading it on the train coming here. Some of my friends lost money in the collapse.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: The letter certainly wasn't addressed to me. Darnley would have called me by name, not 'Dear Sir' - afterall we were friends.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: I don't know whose handkerchief it is.

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: A hard man but very fair. Once I had explained my business problem he agreed to help me out.

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: Darnley should never have married her.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: A charming young lady. Spoiled by her father. Personally I shouldn't like to get on her wrong side.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: Last night was the first time I met him but he seemed a decent fellow.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: I barely know the man.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: I don't know the woman.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: I don't know the girl.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: I don't know the man.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: The bootprints around the house look like mine, but I didn't make them. I'm not flat-footed.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: A cheroot? Not me, old man! I'm one for Havanas if I'm not smoking the old pipe.

text.p: I have worked for the Darnley family since I was a boy. Colonel Darnley was a very honest, decent gentleman. Last night I waited on the guests at dinner. There was the family, Sir Redvers and Mr. Coniston. Afterwards I served port to the gentlemen in the billiard room. I had my own dinner at 9.30 with the other servants, then cleaned boots. I was disturbed by a loud bang at 10.30. I thought it was a door so did nothing about it. Shortly afterwards I went to bed in the butlers' pantry and was woken by Mr. Coniston at 11.30.

He said that he had not been able to sleep and had gone to the lounge to smoke. He saw a light under the study doors and was concerned because the doors were locked and Colonel Darnley would not answer. I offered to get my own keys but Mr. Coniston said it was too urgent. As far as the study is concerned the Colonel kept the keys on his person and I keep mine hidden - I won't say where - but you can be sure no-one can get their on hand either. We left through the kitchen door and I walked round to the french windows, Mr. Coniston ran and was there a couple of seconds before me. He threw a stone through the window and one of us, me I think put his hand through the hole to open the lock. I can confirm that the window was locked. We both went into the room and saw the Colonel's body with a bullet through the chest. The body was on the floor and you couldn't tell where the shot had come from. The Colonel's keys were in the locks of the two doors.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: I was with Mrs. Goodbody and young Norah in my pantry at both 10.30 and 11.30.

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I believe these are Mr. Coniston's slippers. He was wearing them when he woke me up to help the Colonel.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: The boots belong to Sir Redvers. They are - shall we say - on the large side? He gave them to me yesterday afternoon and I cleaned them after dinner. They were in the kitchen until I cleaned them. I suppose they might have been taken afterwards. I don't think anyone could have slipped past us servants to get to the kitchen through my pantry. What puzzles me is : if a boot thief came in from the outside door, what on earth was he wearing on his feet?

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the knife.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I don't smoke. Colonel Darnley smoked cigars.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: It belongs to the master and was kept in the study. The study is normally unlocked and it could have been removed at any time.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: Mr. Mellors told me that a tramp pitched it into the pond two days ago.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: There is a gramophone in the morning room.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: There are two pianos, one in the music room and the other in the morning room.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: I'm not sure. They may be Mrs. Sarah's or Miss Milicent's.

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I'm afraid I don't recognise it.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: The master was extremely good to his family.

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: I don't believe it is proper to speak of the family.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: I don't believe it is proper to speak of the family.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: I scarcely know the gentleman.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I've met the gentleman once or twice. A very hot-tempered man.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: A fine woman. Honest as the day is long.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: A decent-enough girl. A little lazy.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: He's the gamekeeper. He seems honest enough.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I've studied all the people here and none of them are flat-footed. It's a mystery ro me why the bootprints should be flat-footed.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I believe the slipper prints are Mr. Coniston's.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: The shoe prints are mine.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: As far as I'm aware only the Colonel smoked cheroots. But he was a most particular man and would never throw a butt into the pond.

text.p: I have worked here for five years. Last night I cooked and washed up with Norah. At about 10.30 I heard a loud bang, it gave me quite a shock. Not long after we went to bed; me and Norah behind the curtain and Mr. B. on the other side. We had to muck in with Mr. B. because Sir Redvers had turned up unexpectedly and taken our room. I was woke up by a shot at 11.20 (according to my clock); the others was still asleep. I didn't bother 'cos I thought it was poachers. A few minutes later Mr. Coniston turned up in his dressing gown and slippers and woke up Mr. B. but I couldn't hear what it was about.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: We was all three together, me, Norah and Mr. B. in Mr. B.'s pantry at 10.30 and 11.30. Of course we was asleep at 11.30 so I suppose any of us could have slipped out without the others noticing.

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I don't know whose slippers these are.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: I remember Billingsgate cleaning boots after dinner but I can't swear that Sir Redvers' boots were among them. Anyone could have taken the foots from the kitchen if they came in from the outside door. All the outside doors are left open since we live in the country.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I used that knife to skin a hare, which explains the blood.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I don't smoke. Colonel Darnley smoked cigars.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: Mr. B. told me a tramp threw it into the pond. There was a smelly old villain came by a couple of days ago.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: There is a gramophone in the morning room.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: There are two pianos, one in the music room and the other in the morning room.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: I'm not sure. They may be Mrs. Sarah's or Miss Milicent's.

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I don't have time to read the paper.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: Mrs. Sarah and Miss Milicent both have handkerchief's like this one. I can't say whose it is.

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: A hard man both in his business dealings and with his family.

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: Far too young to have married the Colonel and not very happy.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: Not a young woman I should want to cross.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: What a charmer! I always thought he was after Miss Milicent's money, but the Colonel obviously didn't.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I believe he was very cross with the Colonel about some business dealings.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: A gentleman. Very loyal to Colonel Darnley.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: A good girl but lazy.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: Not a nice man. Not to be trusted. Colonel Darnley talked of giving him his notice; he'd already given him a warning letter.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I remember the gardener's child playing around wearing grown-up's boots. They made flat-footed prints because they were too big for her.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

text.p: I have worked here for six months and I don't like it. Last night I helped Mrs. Goodbody to prepare dinner and afterwards I served coffee in the lounge to Mrs. Sarah and Miss. Milicent. Then I helped Mrs. G. to wash up whilst Mr. Billingsgate cleaned the boots. Just before we went to bed there was a loud bang. I thought it was a shot but Mrs. G. said it was a door banging. Then we went to bed. Mrs. G. and I had to sleep behind a curtain in the butlers' pantry 'cos Sir Redvers had come unexpectedly and taken our room. Last thing was I woke when Mr. Coniston woke Mr. B, but I stayed where I was.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: I'm not good where times are concerned. I s'pose I was with Mr. B. and Mrs. G. in Mr. B.'s room.

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I don't know whose slippers these are.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: Mr. B. cleaned boots after dinner.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about the knife.

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: I don't smoke. Colonel Darnley smoked cigars.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: I wasn't aware it was missing.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: There is a gramophone in the morning room.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: There are two pianos, one in the music room and the other in the morning room.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: These are Mrs. Sarah's shoes. i don't know when they were last worn.

interrogation.name: This newspaper
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: Both the ladies have this sort of hanky. Sometimes mrs. G. is given the older ones.

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: Very strict. I didn't like him.

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: If you ask me she didn't like the Colonel. He was too old for her.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: A determined young woman. It's lucky for the Colonel that he agreed to less Miss. Milicent marry Mr. Coniston.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: A very nice gentleman. I'm suprised the Colonel liked him.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I didn't know the gentleman.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: An old fuddy-duddy.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: Very kind to me.

interrogation.name: Ronald Mellors
interrogation.text: A rough man. A womanizer by all accounts.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about them.

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.

text.p: I have worked for Colonel Darnley for five years. He was strict but fair. I have no complaints. Last night I did my last rounds at 8.00 pm. I saw nothing suspicious. I spent the rest of the evening at home and went to bed at 10.00. I didn't hear any strange noises. It wasn't the weather for poachers.

interrogation.name: Alibi
interrogation.text: I was in my cottage alone all evening and I don't gave a tinker's curse if you believe me or not. If you look for my tracks to the big house. you won't find them.

interrogation.name: These Slippers
interrogation.text: I don't give a damn about slippers.

interrogation.name: These Boots
interrogation.text: Piss off! I'm not showing anyone my boots.

interrogation.name: The Knife
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about any bloody knife!

interrogation.name: The Cigarettes
interrogation.text: Go to hell! I'm not answering any questions.

interrogation.name: This Service Revolver
interrogation.text: I don't own a revolver, I use a shotgun.

interrogation.name: The Missing Statue
interrogation.text: Mrs. G. at the big house turned away a tramp. That'd be two days ago. I caught him lurking about and found the statue in the pond.

interrogation.name: The Gramophone
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about any gramophone.

interrogation.name: The Piano
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about any bloody piano.

interrogation.name: These Lady's Shoes
interrogation.text: Do I look like I wear lady's shoes?

interrogation.name: This Newspaper
interrogation.text: I only ever read the racing pages.

interrogation.name: This Letter
interrogation.text: Why should I know anything about some bloody letter?

interrogation.name: This Handkerchief
interrogation.text: Do I look like I use lady's hankys?

interrogation.name: Colonel Darnley
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Sarah Darnley
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Milicent Darnley
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Arthur Coniston
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Sir Redvers Slingsby
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Arnold Billingsgate
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Mildred Goodbody
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Norah Bagsby
interrogation.text: I've nothing to say.

interrogation.name: Flat-footed Bootprints
interrogation.text: Clear off! I'm not showing anyone my boots.

interrogation.name: Hob-nailed Bootprints
interrogation.text: Clear off! I'm not showing anyone my boots.

interrogation.name: Slipper Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose they are.

interrogation.name: Men's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: I don't know whose these are.

interrogation.name: Women's Shoe Prints
interrogation.text: Do I look like I wear women's shoes?

interrogation.name: The Cheroot in the Pond
interrogation.text: I don't know anything about it.
